name: Druj
shortDescription: Druj represents falsehood, deceit, and disorder in Zoroastrianism, opposing the principle of Asha (truth and order). It is a fundamental force of evil and chaos in the cosmos.
description: In Zoroastrianism, Druj embodies the principle of falsehood, deceit, and cosmic disorder. It stands in direct opposition to Asha, the divine principle of truth, righteousness, and cosmic order. Druj is not merely lying; it represents a fundamental distortion of reality, a force that seeks to corrupt and destroy the inherent goodness of creation. It is intimately associated with Angra Mainyu (Ahriman), the source of all evil in the Zoroastrian worldview. The battle between Asha and Druj is a central theme in Zoroastrian cosmology, representing the ongoing struggle between good and evil, order and chaos. Druj manifests in various forms, including moral corruption, physical decay, and cosmic imbalance. Overcoming Druj is a constant task for humanity, requiring adherence to ethical principles, performance of rituals, and unwavering commitment to Asha. The ultimate triumph over Druj is envisioned in the Frashokereti, the renovation of the world at the end of time, where falsehood and evil will be vanquished entirely. The concept is similar to that of Set in Egyptian mythology, or possibly chaos deities in other mythologies, but with distinct Zoroastrian theological nuances. The struggle against Druj is not only a cosmic battle but also an internal one, as individuals must constantly strive to resist the temptations of falsehood and maintain their commitment to truth and righteousness.

sources.title: The Zend-Avesta, Part I (of III): The Vendidad
sources.author: James Darmesteter
